Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside about three hours a day for coursework, but it rarely worked out that neatly. I'm an auditory learner, so I spent a lot of time listening to lecture recordings, sometimes while doing other things. My anxiety made it hard to stick to a schedule because I'd get overwhelmed and then avoid studying altogether for a day or two.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how stressed I was feeling. Some weeks I'd study consistently, but other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was looming. When my anxiety spiked, I'd procrastinate, and then I'd have to cram everything into a few intense sessions. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed to get through.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I remembered an assignment was coming up or when I got an email reminder. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed because the anxiety of not knowing was worse than actually looking. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me curious a few times, which surprised me.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a real habit for me. There were a couple of weeks where I logged in more regularly because I was trying to catch up, but it never felt automatic. My engagement level stayed pretty low because I found it easier to study with downloaded materials than to keep going back to the platform.
Q5: The recorded lectures were by far the most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often play them while taking notes or even while commuting. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts in different ways. I appreciated when instructors responded to questions in the forums too.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They just didn't work for how I learn—I'd rather hear something explained than read pages of text. I also delayed the peer interaction activities because social stuff online makes me anxious, even though I know it's supposed to help.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand something I was confused about or if it counted toward my grade. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they broke things into smaller pieces and gave immediate feedback. That made the work feel less overwhelming for once.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. With my stress levels and everything else going on, I had to prioritize what was required just to keep my head above water. When I did try optional things, I felt guilty for not spending that time on assignments instead.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them offline constantly. I also took handwritten notes that I'd review away from my computer. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over the phone, which helped me process things better than any online activity would.
Q10: A lot of my studying happened while I was listening to downloaded audio files during my commute or while doing chores. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems in my head before I ever opened the LMS. None of that would show up in any log, but it was real learning time for me.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once and then listen to it multiple times over several days without ever logging back in. The same with any PDFs I actually did use—I'd save them to my laptop and work from there. It was just more convenient and less overwhelming than navigating the platform repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement became really hard, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, unable to start anything. I also had some personal stuff going on that drained my energy and made it hard to focus on coursework.
Q13: Workload and fatigue affected me constantly. With ten online courses, I was always juggling something. When I got confused about a topic, I'd sometimes just shut down instead of seeking help because reaching out felt too stressful. My medium resources meant I couldn't always access extra help when I needed it.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxiety about other deadlines or life stuff. I'd technically be logged in and watching, but I wasn't actually learning. It felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments all the time, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes—like if I started and failed, that would be worse than not trying. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same dread.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd often wait until the last possible moment to really engage with materials because the pressure of the deadline was the only thing that could override my anxiety. It wasn't healthy, but the urgency forced me to act when nothing else could.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, but negative feedback sometimes made me spiral and avoid the course for a while. I needed time to process criticism without feeling like a failure. The gamified feedback was easier to handle because it felt less personal.
Q18: The course structure was a bit overwhelming with so many modules and deadlines spread across my ten courses. When things were clearly laid out, I could manage better, but when instructions were vague, my anxiety would take over. I needed more explicit guidance than what was usually provided.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made engagement easier because they felt less intimidating than traditional assignments. But the sheer volume of content and the expectation to participate in discussions made things harder. I wished there were more audio-based options since that's how I learn best.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something specific, or they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Sometimes you log in hoping to feel productive, but the overwhelm stops you from finishing anything. High activity doesn't always mean high productivity—it can mean high anxiety too.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I didn't log in that often, but I did okay on exams because I studied a lot offline. Listening to lectures repeatedly and thinking through concepts in my own time helped more than clicking around the LMS. The platform doesn't capture that kind of deep processing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or how much effort you put in offline. They miss the mental work, the anxiety that slows you down, and the learning that happens away from screens. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and thinking about concepts even when I'm not studying. For me, engagement also means feeling mentally present and not just going through the motions because of anxiety or obligation.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety or how that affected my ability to engage consistently. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures offline or the mental energy it took just to log in some days. My engagement was real, but a lot of it was invisible.
